scenario_blurb: Stage IV colorectal cancer with KRAS G12D mutation. Left-sided and right-sided primary tumors. Treated with FOLFOX or FOLFIRI plus bevacizumab. Some with liver-limited metastases considered for hepatic resection. MSI/MMR testing performed.

events.text: Patient is a 67-year-old male of White/Caucasian ethnicity, non-Hispanic, BMI 26.9, with a history of coronary artery disease (status post stent placement), atrial fibrillation on apixaban, and well-controlled type 2 diabetes.
At age 66, patient presented with weight loss of 15 pounds over 3 months and new iron deficiency anemia; colonoscopy revealed a mass in the ascending colon (C18.2).
Biopsy of ascending colon mass demonstrated poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma (histology 8140/3) with areas of necrosis and high mitotic rate.

Genomic testing showed KRAS G12D mutation, BRAF wild-type, no PIK3CA mutation, TP53 splice site mutation, SMAD4 loss; microsatellite stable (MSS) with intact mismatch repair proteins; tumor mutational burden low (4 mutations/Mb).

CT chest/abdomen/pelvis at age 66 revealed ascending colon mass with adjacent mesenteric lymphadenopathy, innumerable bilobar hepatic metastases (largest 7.2 cm replacing much of right lobe), and bilateral pulmonary metastases (largest 1.8 cm in right lower lobe); staged T4aN2bM1b.
At age 66, baseline CEA was 487 ng/mL and CA 19-9 was 312 U/mL; high-volume metastatic disease with right-sided primary carrying poor prognosis; goals of care discussed.
At age 66, cardiology clearance obtained for systemic therapy; ejection fraction 55% by echocardiogram; bevacizumab considered acceptable given stable cardiac status.
At age 66, patient started first-line FOLFOX plus bevacizumab every 2 weeks with palliative intent.
After cycle 2 of FOLFOX-bevacizumab, patient developed grade 1 proteinuria on routine urinalysis; monitored with urine protein-to-creatinine ratio.
At age 66, patient experienced grade 2 nausea and grade 1 mucositis on FOLFOX, managed with ondansetron and magic mouthwash.
Restaging CT after 6 cycles showed stable disease in hepatic metastases with slight decrease in pulmonary nodules; CEA decreased to 198 ng/mL but remained markedly elevated.
At age 67, CA 19-9 decreased to 187 U/mL; considered best achievable response with ongoing FOLFOX-bevacizumab; plan to continue until progression or toxicity.

At age 67, patient developed grade 2 peripheral neuropathy (oxaliplatin-related) with difficulty buttoning shirts; oxaliplatin discontinued after 10 cycles, continued 5-FU/leucovorin plus bevacizumab maintenance.
CT at age 67 after 14 total cycles (last 4 without oxaliplatin) showed slow progression of hepatic metastases (largest now 8.1 cm) and stable pulmonary nodules; CEA 267 ng/mL.
At age 67, transitioned to second-line FOLFIRI plus bevacizumab upon progression on FOLFOX-based regimen.
At age 67, patient developed grade 2 diarrhea on FOLFIRI with 5-6 loose stools daily, managed with loperamide.
CT after 4 cycles of FOLFIRI-bevacizumab showed continued slow progression; CEA 334 ng/mL; patient maintained ECOG 1 performance status.
At age 67, apixaban was held perioperatively when patient required percutaneous biliary drainage for biliary obstruction from hepatic metastases.
At age 67, given progressive disease on two lines of therapy, discussed TAS-102 or regorafenib as third-line options; patient elected TAS-102 given hand-foot syndrome concerns with regorafenib.
At age 67, patient started TAS-102 (trifluridine/tipiracil) as third-line therapy with continued bevacizumab.
At age 67, palliative care co-management initiated for pain control and symptom management; patient's goals remain quality of life with continued anti-cancer therapy.

documents.text:
PATHOLOGY REPORT

SPECIMEN ID: S24-07832

TYPE OF PROCEDURE: Colonoscopic biopsy

TYPE OF SPECIMEN: Ascending colon mass, biopsy (4 fragments)

CLINICAL HISTORY: 66-year-old male with 15 lb unintentional weight loss over 3 months and iron deficiency anemia. Colonoscopy identified a large partially obstructing mass in the ascending colon.

GROSS PATHOLOGY:
Received in formalin labeled "ascending colon mass" are four fragments of tan-pink to tan-brown tissue, the largest measuring 0.6 x 0.4 x 0.3 cm and the smallest measuring 0.3 x 0.2 x 0.2 cm. All fragments submitted entirely in one cassette.

MICROSCOPIC/DIAGNOSTIC FINDINGS:
Sections show colonic mucosa infiltrated by a poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma. The tumor is composed of irregular glandular structures and solid sheets of cells with marked nuclear pleomorphism, vesicular chromatin, and prominent nucleoli. There is brisk mitotic activity (>20 mitoses per 10 high-power fields) including atypical forms. Extensive areas of tumor necrosis involving approximately 40% of the viable tissue are identified. The tumor infiltrates through the muscularis mucosae into the submucosa. Lymphovascular invasion is present. Perineural invasion is not definitively identified on this limited biopsy material.

Histologic type: Adenocarcinoma, NOS (8140/3)
Histologic grade: Poorly differentiated (Grade 3)

ANCILLARY STUDIES:
Immunohistochemistry for mismatch repair proteins:
- MLH1: Intact nuclear expression
- PMS2: Intact nuclear expression
- MSH2: Intact nuclear expression
- MSH6: Intact nuclear expression
Interpretation: Intact mismatch repair protein expression (no loss detected). This result does not exclude Lynch syndrome; clinical correlation and genetic counseling recommended if indicated.

CDX2: Positive (diffuse, strong)
CK20: Positive
CK7: Negative
Immunohistochemical profile is consistent with colorectal primary.

COMMENT:
The morphologic and immunohistochemical features are consistent with a colorectal primary poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma. The intact mismatch repair protein expression indicates microsatellite stable (MSS) status, which correlates with a low likelihood of response to immune checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy. Molecular testing for RAS/BRAF mutations is recommended to guide systemic therapy selection.

NEXT-GENERATION SEQUENCING REPORT

SPECIMEN INFORMATION:
Specimen type: Formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded tissue
Specimen site: Ascending colon, biopsy
Specimen ID: S24-07832
Tumor content: 60%
Sequencing panel: Comprehensive Genomic Profiling (324 genes)
Sequencing platform: Illumina NovaSeq 6000
Mean coverage depth: 583x

DIAGNOSIS: Poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma of the ascending colon

GENOMIC FINDINGS:

1. KRAS p.G12D (c.35G>A) - Pathogenic
   Variant allele frequency: 34%
   Exon 2, codon 12 missense mutation
   Clinical significance: Activating mutation in the KRAS oncogene. Predicts lack of benefit from anti-EGFR monoclonal antibodies (cetuximab, panitumumab). Tumor is eligible for standard chemotherapy backbone regimens with anti-VEGF therapy.

2. TP53 c.672+1G>A (splice site) - Pathogenic
   Variant allele frequency: 41%
   Intron 6 splice donor site mutation predicted to cause exon 6 skipping and loss of function.
   Clinical significance: Loss-of-function alteration in the TP53 tumor suppressor gene. Commonly observed in colorectal adenocarcinomas. No directly targetable therapies currently approved.

3. SMAD4 - Loss (copy number loss)
   Estimated copy number: 0
   Clinical significance: Biallelic loss of the SMAD4 tumor suppressor, a component of TGF-beta signaling. Associated with more aggressive disease behavior in colorectal cancer and has been correlated with increased propensity for distant metastases.

NEGATIVE FINDINGS (selected clinically relevant genes):
- BRAF: No mutation detected (wild-type at V600 and other hotspots)
- PIK3CA: No mutation detected
- NRAS: No mutation detected
- HER2 (ERBB2): No amplification, no activating mutation
- APC: Frameshift mutation detected (see VUS section)
- PTEN: No mutation or loss detected
- MET: No amplification

MICROSATELLITE INSTABILITY STATUS: Microsatellite Stable (MSS)
Genomic method: Assessment of 114 intronic homopolymer loci. Result concordant with immunohistochemistry showing intact MMR protein expression.

TUMOR MUTATIONAL BURDEN: 4 mutations/Mb (Low)
Interpretation: Low TMB. In conjunction with MSS status, low likelihood of meaningful response to PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy.

VARIANTS OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE:

1. APC p.R1450* (c.4348C>T) - Likely pathogenic / VUS
   Variant allele frequency: 38%
   Nonsense mutation. APC truncating mutations are ubiquitous in sporadic colorectal cancer and considered early driver events. Listed as VUS pending final annotation.

2. FBXW7 p.R465C (c.1393C>T) - VUS
   Variant allele frequency: 12%
   Missense variant in a known hotspot. May contribute to impaired protein degradation via the ubiquitin-proteasome pathway. Clinical significance uncertain.

3. ATM p.V2424G (c.7271T>G) - VUS
   Variant allele frequency: 22%
   Missense variant. Functional impact uncertain; no known association with therapeutic sensitivity in colorectal cancer.

RADIOLOGY REPORT

STUDY TYPE: CT Chest, Abdomen, and Pelvis with IV contrast

CLINICAL INDICATION: Newly diagnosed ascending colon adenocarcinoma. Staging evaluation.

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 intravenous contrast. Oral contrast was administered. Images were acquired in the portal venous phase with 3 mm axial and coronal/sagittal reformations.

FINDINGS:

Chest:
Lungs and airways: Multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules consistent with metastatic disease. In the right lower lobe, the dominant nodule measures 1.8 x 1.5 cm (axial image 47). Additional right lower lobe nodule measuring 0.9 cm. Left lower lobe nodules measuring 1.2 cm and 0.7 cm. Left upper lobe nodule measuring 0.6 cm. Total of at least 8 discrete pulmonary nodules identified. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax.
Mediastinum: No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes by size criteria. Heart size is normal. Coronary artery calcifications noted. No pericardial effusion.
Chest wall: Degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. No suspicious osseous lesions.

Abdomen:
Liver: Innumerable bilobar hepatic metastases are identified, too numerous to count. The largest lesion is in segment VII/VIII measuring 7.2 x 5.8 cm with peripheral enhancement and central hypodensity consistent with necrosis. This lesion replaces much of the posterior right hepatic lobe. Additional dominant lesions include a 4.3 cm lesion in segment IVa, a 3.1 cm lesion in segment II, and a 2.8 cm lesion in segment VI. The estimated hepatic tumor burden is approximately 40-50% of the liver parenchyma. The intrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. Portal vein is patent.
Gallbladder and biliary tree: Unremarkable. No cholelithiasis.
Pancreas: Normal in size and attenuation. No pancreatic ductal dilation.
Spleen: Normal size at 11.2 cm. No splenic lesions.
Adrenal glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are normal.
Kidneys: Bilateral kidneys demonstrate normal enhancement. Simple cortical cyst in the left kidney measuring 1.4 cm. No hydronephrosis.
Ascending colon: Irregular circumferential wall thickening of the ascending colon extending over an approximately 6 cm segment with luminal narrowing. The mass appears to extend through the serosa into the pericolic fat. There is associated pericolic fat stranding.
Lymph nodes: Multiple enlarged mesenteric lymph nodes along the ileocolic artery, the largest measuring 2.4 x 1.8 cm. Additional borderline mesenteric nodes. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.
Peritoneum: No ascites. No omental caking or peritoneal nodularity to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis.

Pelvis:
Bladder: Normal distension. No wall thickening.
Prostate: Mildly enlarged, likely benign prostatic hypertrophy.
No pelvic lymphadenopathy.
No suspicious osseous lesions.

Bone: No lytic or blastic osseous metastases identified. Degenerative changes of the lumbar spine.

IMPRESSION:
1. Large ascending colon mass with extraserosal extension, consistent with known primary adenocarcinoma. Staged at least T4a by imaging criteria.
2. Multiple enlarged mesenteric lymph nodes along the ileocolic arterial distribution, consistent with nodal metastases (N2b).
3. Innumerable bilobar hepatic metastases with high tumor burden (estimated 40-50% hepatic replacement), largest 7.2 cm in segment VII/VIII. Disease is not amenable to hepatic resection.
4. Multiple bilateral pulmonary metastases, largest 1.8 cm in the right lower lobe.
5. Overall stage: T4a N2b M1b (hepatic and pulmonary metastases).

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Newly diagnosed metastatic colon cancer, initial medical oncology consultation.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
This is a 66-year-old gentleman who is seen today for initial medical oncology consultation for newly diagnosed metastatic adenocarcinoma of the ascending colon. The patient first noticed progressive unintentional weight loss of approximately 15 pounds over the past 3 months. He also developed fatigue and was found to have iron deficiency anemia on laboratory workup by his primary care physician (hemoglobin 9.4 g/dL, ferritin 8 ng/mL, iron sat 9%). A colonoscopy was performed and revealed a large, partially obstructing mass in the ascending colon. Biopsies demonstrated poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma, grade 3, with intact mismatch repair proteins by immunohistochemistry (MLH1, PMS2, MSH2, MSH6 all intact), indicating microsatellite stable disease.

Next-generation sequencing returned with KRAS G12D mutation, TP53 splice site mutation, and SMAD4 loss. BRAF wild-type. Tumor mutational burden low at 4 mut/Mb.

Staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis shows the ascending colon mass with extraserosal extension (T4a), multiple enlarged mesenteric lymph nodes (N2b), innumerable bilobar hepatic metastases with the largest measuring 7.2 cm replacing much of the right hepatic lobe, and bilateral pulmonary metastases with the largest measuring 1.8 cm in the right lower lobe. Overall stage T4aN2bM1b.

Baseline tumor markers are markedly elevated: CEA 487 ng/mL and CA 19-9 312 U/mL.

The patient reports continued weight loss, reduced appetite, and fatigue. He has occasional right-sided abdominal discomfort but no acute obstruction. No nausea, vomiting, or change in bowel habits aside from slightly looser stools. No rectal bleeding.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Weight loss of 15 lbs, fatigue, decreased appetite. No fevers or night sweats.
Cardiovascular: Occasional palpitations (known atrial fibrillation). No chest pain, dyspnea on exertion at baseline activity level.
Gastrointestinal: Mild right-sided abdominal discomfort. Slightly loose stools. No nausea, vomiting, hematemesis, melena, or bright red blood per rectum.
Neurologic: No headaches, vision changes, numbness, or tingling.
Musculoskeletal: No bone pain.
All other systems reviewed and negative.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Coronary artery disease, status post drug-eluting stent to the left anterior descending artery (4 years ago)
2. Atrial fibrillation, chronic, on rate control and anticoagulation
3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus, well-controlled on metformin
4. Hypertension
5. Hyperlipidemia
6. Benign prostatic hypertrophy

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:
1. Percutaneous coronary intervention with stent placement
2. Appendectomy (remote)

MEDICATIONS:
1. Apixaban (Eliquis) 5 mg twice daily
2. Metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily
3. Metformin 1000 mg twice daily
4. Atorvastatin 40 mg daily
5. Lisinopril 10 mg daily
6. Aspirin 81 mg daily (held per cardiology)
7. Tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily

ALLERGIES: Sulfonamides (rash)

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Retired electrician. Lives with wife. Former smoker, quit 12 years ago, approximately 20 pack-year history. Social alcohol use, approximately 2-3 beers per week, decreased recently due to feeling unwell. No illicit drug use. Has two adult children who are supportive and involved in care.

FAMILY HISTORY:
Father: Died of myocardial infarction at age 72
Mother: Breast cancer diagnosed at age 74, survived
Siblings: One brother with type 2 diabetes
No known family history of colorectal cancer or Lynch syndrome-associated cancers.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: BP 134/78, HR 76 (irregular), Temp 98.2F, SpO2 97% on room air, Weight 187 lbs (BMI 26.9)
General: Alert, oriented, well-groomed male appearing stated age. Mild cachexia. ECOG performance status 1.
HEENT: Sclera anicteric. Oral mucosa moist without lesions. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Irregularly irregular rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, crackles, or rhonchi.
Abdomen: Soft, mildly distended. Firm, palpable mass in the right lower quadrant/right flank region. Mild tenderness to deep palpation over the mass. Liver edge palpable 4 cm below the right costal margin, firm and nodular. No splenomegaly. No ascites by exam.
Skin: No jaundice. No rashes. No petechiae.
Neurologic: No focal deficits. Sensation intact.
Extremities: No edema. No cyanosis.

LAB RESULTS:
WBC 8.7 x10^9/L, Hemoglobin 9.1 g/dL, Platelets 342 x10^9/L
Creatinine 1.02 mg/dL, BUN 18 mg/dL
Total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL, ALT 52 U/L, AST 61 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 187 U/L, Albumin 3.2 g/dL
LDH 312 U/L
CEA 487 ng/mL, CA 19-9 312 U/mL
Glucose 142 mg/dL, HbA1c 6.8%

IMAGING RESULTS:
CT C/A/P: As described above. T4aN2bM1b with high-volume hepatic disease (innumerable bilobar metastases, largest 7.2 cm) and bilateral pulmonary metastases.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:
66-year-old gentleman with newly diagnosed stage IV (T4aN2bM1b) poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma of the ascending colon with KRAS G12D mutation, MSS, BRAF wild-type, with high-volume bilobar hepatic metastases and bilateral pulmonary metastases.

This is a right-sided primary, which carries a worse prognosis compared with left-sided colorectal cancers. The KRAS G12D mutation precludes the use of anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab or panitumumab). MSS status with low TMB makes immunotherapy unlikely to be effective as single agent. The high hepatic tumor burden makes this unresectable, so treatment intent is palliative.

1. Recommend first-line FOLFOX (5-FU, leucovorin, oxaliplatin) plus bevacizumab (Avastin) every 14 days. This regimen offers the best balance of efficacy and tolerability for this clinical scenario.
2. Will need cardiology clearance given history of CAD and atrial fibrillation prior to starting bevacizumab. Have placed referral to Dr. Patel in cardiology.
3. Goals of care discussion was held today with the patient and his wife. I explained that this is a stage IV cancer that is not curable, but that chemotherapy can help control the disease, improve symptoms, and prolong life. Median survival with treatment is approximately 24-30 months. The patient understands the diagnosis and prognosis and wishes to proceed with aggressive systemic therapy.
4. Iron deficiency anemia -- will start IV iron sucrose prior to chemotherapy initiation. Transfuse if hemoglobin drops below 7.5 g/dL.
5. Continue apixaban for atrial fibrillation. Will discuss with cardiology regarding management during any procedures.
6. Diabetes is well-controlled. Will monitor glucose on steroids with chemotherapy.
7. Will obtain port placement prior to starting chemotherapy.
8. Follow-up in 1-2 weeks after cardiology clearance and port placement to initiate treatment.

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Cardiology clearance for initiation of chemotherapy with bevacizumab.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
This is a 66-year-old gentleman with known coronary artery disease status post DES to the LAD 4 years ago and chronic atrial fibrillation, referred by medical oncology for cardiac clearance prior to initiation of FOLFOX plus bevacizumab for newly diagnosed stage IV adenocarcinoma of the ascending colon. He reports no chest pain, no worsening dyspnea, and stable exercise tolerance. He walks approximately half a mile daily without significant limitation, limited more recently by cancer-related fatigue than cardiac symptoms. He denies orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, or lower extremity swelling.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Cardiovascular: No chest pain or pressure. Occasional palpitations consistent with known AF. No syncope or presyncope. No lower extremity edema.
Respiratory: No dyspnea at rest. Mild exertional fatigue.
All other systems reviewed and negative except as noted in oncology records.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: BP 128/74, HR 72 (irregular), Temp 98.4F, SpO2 98% on room air
General: Pleasant male, mild cachexia, in no distress.
Cardiovascular: Irregularly irregular rhythm. Normal S1, S2. No S3, S4, murmurs, or rubs. JVP not elevated. No peripheral edema.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Extremities: Warm, well-perfused. No edema. Distal pulses 2+ bilaterally.

LAB RESULTS:
BNP: 89 pg/mL (within normal limits)
Troponin I: <0.01 ng/mL (normal)

IMAGING RESULTS:
Echocardiogram: Left ventricular ejection fraction 55%. Normal LV wall thickness. No regional wall motion abnormalities. Mild left atrial enlargement consistent with atrial fibrillation. Trace mitral regurgitation. Normal right ventricular size and function. No pericardial effusion.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:
1. Coronary artery disease, stable: Patient is 4 years out from PCI with no recurrent anginal symptoms. Ejection fraction preserved at 55%. Acceptable cardiac risk for systemic chemotherapy.
2. Atrial fibrillation: Rate well-controlled on metoprolol. Continue apixaban anticoagulation. Oncology team aware that apixaban will need to be managed around any procedures.
3. Bevacizumab: The patient's cardiac status is acceptable for bevacizumab therapy. His blood pressure is reasonably controlled. LVEF is preserved. I have counseled that bevacizumab can cause hypertension and rare arterial thromboembolic events, and that his CAD history places him at slightly higher baseline risk, but the benefit-risk ratio favors treatment given his oncologic situation. Will need blood pressure monitoring at each infusion visit.
4. Will follow up in 3 months or sooner if cardiac symptoms develop.

RADIOLOGY REPORT

STUDY TYPE: CT Chest, Abdomen, and Pelvis with IV contrast

CLINICAL INDICATION: Metastatic colon adenocarcinoma on FOLFOX plus bevacizumab. Restaging after 6 cycles.

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following administration of 95 mL of Omnipaque 350 intravenous contrast. Oral contrast was administered. Comparison: CT from approximately 3 months prior.

FINDINGS:

Chest:
Lungs: The previously identified bilateral pulmonary metastases demonstrate interval slight decrease in size. The dominant right lower lobe nodule now measures 1.4 x 1.2 cm, previously 1.8 x 1.5 cm. Left lower lobe nodule measures 0.9 cm, previously 1.2 cm. Left upper lobe nodule stable at 0.6 cm. The remaining small nodules are stable to mildly decreased. No new pulmonary nodules.
Mediastinum: No new lymphadenopathy. Heart size stable. Coronary calcifications again noted.
Pleura: No effusion.

Abdomen:
Liver: The innumerable bilobar hepatic metastases are grossly stable in size and number. The dominant lesion in segment VII/VIII measures 7.0 x 5.6 cm, previously 7.2 x 5.8 cm, representing a minimal decrease within measurement variability. The segment IVa lesion is stable at 4.2 cm. Segment II lesion stable at 3.0 cm. Segment VI lesion stable at 2.7 cm. No new hepatic lesions identified. No new intrahepatic biliary dilation.
Gallbladder and biliary: Unremarkable.
Pancreas: Unremarkable.
Spleen: Normal.
Adrenals: Normal.
Kidneys: Stable left renal cyst. No hydronephrosis.
Colon: The ascending colon mass appears grossly stable. No evidence of obstruction. Mild surrounding fat stranding, unchanged.
Lymph nodes: The mesenteric lymph nodes are stable, the largest measuring 2.2 cm.
Peritoneum: No ascites. No peritoneal implants.

Pelvis:
No significant change from prior.

Bone: No new osseous lesions.

IMPRESSION:
1. Stable hepatic metastatic disease. The dominant right hepatic lesion shows a minimal decrease from 7.2 to 7.0 cm, within measurement variability.
2. Mild decrease in bilateral pulmonary metastases, most notable in the dominant right lower lobe nodule (1.8 to 1.4 cm).
3. Stable ascending colon primary mass and mesenteric lymphadenopathy.
4. Overall assessment: Stable disease by RECIST 1.1 criteria.

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Follow-up, metastatic colon cancer on FOLFOX plus bevacizumab.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
This 67-year-old gentleman returns to medical oncology clinic for follow-up. He has completed 8 cycles of FOLFOX plus Avastin for stage IV adenocarcinoma of the ascending colon with liver and lung metastases. Restaging CT after cycle 6 showed stable hepatic disease and slight improvement in pulmonary metastases, consistent with stable disease by RECIST 1.1. CEA has decreased from 487 to 198 ng/mL and CA 19-9 has decreased from 312 to 187 U/mL.

The patient reports that his energy level has improved somewhat compared to before starting treatment. His weight has stabilized at 185 lbs. He continues to have intermittent nausea, managed with ondansetron, and mild oral discomfort from mucositis that has improved with magic mouthwash. He reports tingling in his fingertips that started about 2 cycles ago and is mild, not interfering with daily activities currently. Blood pressure has been running slightly higher at home (140s-150s systolic) since starting Avastin; lisinopril was uptitrated at last visit.

No new significant complaints. Bowel function is regular. No rectal bleeding. Appetite is fair.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Weight stable. Fatigue improved from baseline. No fevers.
Gastrointestinal: Intermittent mild nausea. Mild oral soreness improving. No vomiting, diarrhea, or constipation.
Neurologic: Mild fingertip tingling bilaterally. No difficulty with fine motor tasks currently.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain. Blood pressure running higher at home.
All other systems negative.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: BP 142/82, HR 74 (irregular), Temp 98.0F, SpO2 97% on room air, Weight 185 lbs
General: Alert, appears well, mild cachexia improved from prior visits. ECOG 1.
HEENT: Oral mucosa with mild erythema, no ulcerations. No thrush.
Cardiovascular: Irregularly irregular. No new murmurs.
Respiratory: Clear bilaterally.
Abdomen: Soft, mildly distended. Liver edge palpable 3 cm below costal margin, unchanged. Mild RLQ fullness. Nontender.
Extremities: No edema. Sensation intact to light touch in fingers and toes. No motor deficits.
Skin: No rash. Port site clean and well-healed.

LAB RESULTS:
WBC 5.8 x10^9/L, Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, Platelets 218 x10^9/L
Creatinine 1.05 mg/dL, ALT 44 U/L, AST 48 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 162 U/L, Albumin 3.4 g/dL
CEA 198 ng/mL, CA 19-9 187 U/mL
Urine protein/creatinine ratio: 0.18 (normal <0.2)

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:
1. Stage IV colon adenocarcinoma (ascending, KRAS G12D, MSS) -- stable disease on FOLFOX-bevacizumab with declining tumor markers. This represents the best achievable response given the high tumor burden. Plan to continue current regimen until disease progression or dose-limiting toxicity.
2. Nausea (grade 1, improved) -- continue ondansetron PRN. Consider adding olanzapine 5 mg if worsens.
3. Mucositis (grade 1, improving) -- continue magic mouthwash. Resolving.
4. Peripheral neuropathy (grade 1, oxaliplatin) -- currently mild fingertip tingling without functional impairment. Will monitor closely. If progresses to grade 2, will discontinue oxaliplatin and continue 5-FU/LV plus bevacizumab maintenance.
5. Hypertension (bevacizumab-related) -- increased lisinopril to 20 mg daily at last visit. BP still elevated today. Will add amlodipine 5 mg daily. Goal <140/90.
6. Proteinuria (grade 1) -- urine protein/creatinine ratio borderline. Continue monitoring each cycle.
7. Anemia -- hemoglobin improved to 10.2 from 9.1 with IV iron. No transfusion needed.
8. Continue all other medications as currently prescribed.
9. Return in 2 weeks for cycle 9. Restaging CT in approximately 6-8 weeks.

RADIOLOGY REPORT

STUDY TYPE: CT Chest, Abdomen, and Pelvis with IV contrast

CLINICAL INDICATION: Metastatic colon adenocarcinoma. Currently on 5-FU/leucovorin plus bevacizumab maintenance (oxaliplatin discontinued for neuropathy after 10 cycles). Restaging.

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed with 100 mL Omnipaque 350 IV contrast and oral contrast. Comparison: CT from approximately 3 months prior.

FINDINGS:

Chest:
Lungs: Bilateral pulmonary nodules are stable to minimally changed. Right lower lobe nodule measures 1.3 cm, previously 1.4 cm. Left lower lobe nodule measures 1.0 cm, previously 0.9 cm. Remaining nodules grossly stable. No new pulmonary nodules. No pleural effusion.

Abdomen:
Liver: The hepatic metastases demonstrate interval slow growth. The dominant segment VII/VIII lesion now measures 8.1 x 6.4 cm, previously 7.0 x 5.6 cm, representing approximately 15% increase in longest diameter. The segment IVa lesion measures 4.9 cm, previously 4.2 cm. The segment II lesion measures 3.5 cm, previously 3.0 cm. The segment VI lesion measures 3.2 cm, previously 2.7 cm. Two possible new subcentimeter hepatic lesions in segment V (0.8 cm) and segment III (0.6 cm), too small to characterize but suspicious in this clinical context. No intrahepatic biliary dilation at this time.
Gallbladder: Unremarkable.
Pancreas: Unremarkable.
Spleen: Normal.
Kidneys: Stable left renal cyst. No hydronephrosis.
Colon: Ascending colon mass appears slightly larger, now measuring approximately 6.5 cm in craniocaudal extent versus 6 cm previously. No evidence of obstruction.
Lymph nodes: The dominant mesenteric lymph node measures 2.6 cm, previously 2.2 cm. Additional mesenteric nodes appear slightly larger.
Peritoneum: Trace free fluid in the pelvis, new. No omental caking.

Pelvis:
Bladder: Normal. Small-volume pelvic ascites.
No pelvic lymphadenopathy.

Bone: No new osseous lesions.

IMPRESSION:
1. Interval growth of hepatic metastases with the dominant lesion increasing from 7.0 to 8.1 cm. Two possible new subcentimeter hepatic lesions suspicious for new metastatic foci.
2. Mild interval growth of mesenteric lymphadenopathy.
3. Slight growth of the ascending colon primary mass.
4. Bilateral pulmonary metastases are stable.
5. New trace pelvic free fluid.
6. Overall assessment: Progressive disease by RECIST 1.1 criteria (unequivocal progression of non-target hepatic disease and growth of measurable lesions exceeding 20% sum of longest diameters).

RADIOLOGY REPORT

STUDY TYPE: CT Chest, Abdomen, and Pelvis with IV contrast

CLINICAL INDICATION: Metastatic colon adenocarcinoma on second-line FOLFIRI plus bevacizumab. Restaging after 4 cycles.

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed with 95 mL Omnipaque 350 IV contrast and oral contrast. Comparison: CT from approximately 2 months prior.

FINDINGS:

Chest:
Lungs: The bilateral pulmonary nodules are grossly stable. Right lower lobe nodule measures 1.4 cm, previously 1.3 cm. Left lower lobe nodule stable at 1.0 cm. No new pulmonary nodules. No pleural effusion.

Abdomen:
Liver: Continued slow progression of hepatic metastatic disease. The dominant segment VII/VIII lesion measures 8.6 x 6.8 cm, previously 8.1 x 6.4 cm. Segment IVa lesion measures 5.2 cm, previously 4.9 cm. Segment II lesion measures 3.7 cm, previously 3.5 cm. The previously noted subcentimeter lesions in segment V and segment III have grown to 1.2 cm and 0.9 cm respectively, confirming metastatic disease. There is now mild intrahepatic biliary dilation of the left hepatic duct, with the segment IVa mass abutting the confluence. Common bile duct measures 0.9 cm, upper limits of normal.
Gallbladder: Mildly distended but otherwise unremarkable.
Pancreas: Unremarkable.
Spleen: Normal.
Kidneys: Stable left renal cyst.
Colon: Ascending colon mass appears stable.
Lymph nodes: Mesenteric lymphadenopathy is stable, largest 2.7 cm.
Peritoneum: Small-volume ascites in the pelvis and bilateral paracolic gutters, increased from trace fluid on prior.

Pelvis:
Bladder: Normal. Small-volume pelvic ascites.
No pelvic lymphadenopathy.

Bone: No new osseous lesions.

IMPRESSION:
1. Continued slow progression of hepatic metastases. Dominant lesion now 8.6 cm. Previously indeterminate subcentimeter hepatic lesions have grown, confirming additional metastatic foci.
2. New mild left intrahepatic biliary dilation, likely from mass effect of segment IVa metastasis at the ductal confluence. Should be monitored for progressive obstruction.
3. Increasing small-volume ascites.
4. Pulmonary metastases are stable.
5. Overall: Continued progressive disease on FOLFIRI-bevacizumab.

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Progressive metastatic colon cancer, discussion of third-line therapy.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
This 67-year-old gentleman returns to discuss treatment options. He has now progressed through two lines of systemic therapy for his KRAS G12D mutant, MSS, stage IV ascending colon adenocarcinoma. He received first-line FOLFOX plus bevacizumab (10 cycles with oxaliplatin, then 4 additional cycles with 5-FU/LV plus bevacizumab maintenance), achieving stable disease as best response. He then received second-line FOLFIRI plus bevacizumab for 4 cycles with continued slow progression of hepatic metastases.

Recent CT shows continued growth of hepatic metastases (dominant lesion now 8.6 cm), mild left intrahepatic biliary dilation from the segment IVa metastasis, increasing small-volume ascites, and stable pulmonary nodules. CEA has risen to 334 ng/mL. He recently required percutaneous biliary drainage for symptomatic biliary obstruction; the drain is functioning well.

The patient reports progressive fatigue and decreased appetite. He has lost an additional 8 lbs over the past 2 months (current weight 177 lbs). Right upper quadrant discomfort is intermittent and managed with acetaminophen. He has grade 2 diarrhea on FOLFIRI (5-6 loose stools/day) managed with loperamide, and residual grade 2 peripheral neuropathy from prior oxaliplatin (difficulty with buttons and zippers). His ECOG performance status remains 1.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Weight loss, fatigue, decreased appetite. No fevers.
Gastrointestinal: Diarrhea as above. RUQ discomfort. No nausea or vomiting currently. Biliary drain functioning.
Neurologic: Persistent fingertip and toe numbness/tingling. Difficulty with fine motor tasks.
Musculoskeletal: Mild generalized weakness.
All other systems negative.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: BP 136/76, HR 78 (irregular), Temp 98.6F, SpO2 96% on room air, Weight 177 lbs
General: Cachectic male, appearing older than stated age. Fatigued but alert. ECOG 1.
HEENT: Mild scleral icterus. Oral mucosa dry. No lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Irregularly irregular. No murmurs.
Respiratory: Decreased breath sounds at the right base. No wheezes.
Abdomen: Distended. Hepatomegaly with liver edge 5 cm below costal margin, firm and nodular. Mild RUQ tenderness. Percutaneous biliary drain in the right flank, site clean, no erythema, draining bile. Mild shifting dullness concerning for ascites.
Extremities: 1+ bilateral lower extremity edema, new. Decreased sensation to light touch in fingertips and toes bilaterally.
Skin: Mild jaundice. No rashes.

LAB RESULTS:
WBC 4.2 x10^9/L, ANC 2.1 x10^9/L, Hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL, Platelets 168 x10^9/L
Creatinine 1.18 mg/dL, BUN 24 mg/dL
Total bilirubin 2.4 mg/dL, Direct bilirubin 1.8 mg/dL, ALT 68 U/L, AST 82 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 298 U/L, Albumin 2.8 g/dL
LDH 445 U/L
CEA 334 ng/mL, CA 19-9 278 U/mL

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:
1. Stage IV ascending colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D, MSS), progressive disease on two lines of therapy.
   - Discussed third-line options:
     a) TAS-102 (Lonsurf, trifluridine/tipiracil): Oral agent, shown to improve overall survival vs placebo in refractory mCRC. Common toxicities include myelosuppression, fatigue, nausea. Recent data supports combination with bevacizumab (SUNLIGHT trial).
     b) Regorafenib (Stivarga): Oral multikinase inhibitor. Survival benefit demonstrated in refractory mCRC. Notable toxicities include hand-foot skin reaction, hypertension, hepatotoxicity, fatigue.
   - Given the patient's existing neuropathy making hand discomfort undesirable, and his preference to avoid hand-foot syndrome, patient elects TAS-102. Will combine with continued bevacizumab based on SUNLIGHT data.
   - KRAS G12D is not amenable to sotorasib (approved for G12C only). No other actionable mutations.
   - Not a candidate for immunotherapy given MSS status and low TMB.
2. Biliary obstruction -- biliary drain functioning. Bilirubin trending down from peak of 4.1 to current 2.4. Will need bilirubin below 1.5 ideally before starting TAS-102.
3. Declining nutrition and weight loss -- initiating palliative care co-management for symptom optimization. Nutritional counseling referral placed.
4. Peripheral neuropathy (grade 2, oxaliplatin-related) -- ongoing. Gabapentin 300 mg TID started.
5. Anemia -- chronic, multifactorial (disease, chemotherapy, chronic disease). Continue to monitor, transfuse if <7.5.
6. New bilateral lower extremity edema -- likely hypoalbuminemia. Will monitor. Compression stockings recommended.
7. Plan to start TAS-102 plus bevacizumab once bilirubin normalizes, estimated in 1-2 weeks.
8. Return in 1 week for labs and reassessment.

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Palliative care initial consultation for pain and symptom management.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
This is a 67-year-old gentleman with stage IV ascending colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D, MSS) with extensive hepatic and pulmonary metastases, referred by medical oncology for palliative care co-management. He has progressed through FOLFOX-bevacizumab and FOLFIRI-bevacizumab, and is now starting third-line TAS-102 plus bevacizumab. He recently required percutaneous biliary drainage for biliary obstruction from hepatic metastases.

His primary symptoms are:
1. Right upper quadrant and right flank pain, rated 5/10 at baseline, intermittently spiking to 7/10, particularly with movement and deep breathing. Currently managed with acetaminophen 1000 mg every 6 hours with incomplete relief.
2. Fatigue, progressive, limiting activity to approximately 2-3 hours of meaningful activity per day.
3. Decreased appetite and early satiety, likely related to hepatomegaly and ascites.
4. Diarrhea (5-6 episodes daily on FOLFIRI, now holding), managed with loperamide.
5. Numbness and tingling in hands and feet from prior oxaliplatin, interfering with fine motor tasks.

The patient is fully aware of his diagnosis and prognosis. He has discussed goals of care with his oncologist. His goals are to maintain quality of life while continuing cancer-directed therapy for as long as it provides benefit. He wants to remain at home as much as possible. He is not ready to discuss hospice at this time. He has an advance directive naming his wife as healthcare proxy.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: 23 lb total weight loss since diagnosis. Progressive fatigue. Poor appetite.
Pain: As described above.
Gastrointestinal: Early satiety. Intermittent nausea without vomiting. Diarrhea improving off FOLFIRI.
Psychological: Mild anxiety about disease progression. Occasional low mood. Sleep disrupted by pain, waking 2-3 times nightly.
Neurologic: Neuropathic symptoms as described.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: BP 130/72, HR 80 (irregular), Weight 177 lbs
General: Cachectic gentleman, appears fatigued but engaged. ECOG 1-2.
Abdomen: Distended, hepatomegaly with firm nodular liver edge palpable. Biliary drain in right flank intact. Mild diffuse tenderness, worst in RUQ. Mild ascites.
Extremities: 1+ bilateral LE edema. Decreased sensation in glove-and-stocking distribution.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:
1. Cancer-related pain (hepatic capsular distension and abdominal distension):
   - Start low-dose oxycodone 5 mg PO every 4-6 hours PRN for moderate-severe pain
   - Continue scheduled acetaminophen 1000 mg every 6 hours (max 3g/day given liver disease)
   - If requiring >3 doses oxycodone/day, will transition to sustained-release opioid
   - Bowel regimen: senna-docusate 1 tab twice daily to prevent opioid-induced constipation (adjust given concurrent diarrhea tendency)
2. Neuropathic pain (oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy):
   - Gabapentin 300 mg TID recently started by oncology. Titrate to 600 mg TID as tolerated over the next 2 weeks.
   - If insufficient, consider duloxetine as adjunct
3. Anorexia/cachexia:
   - Start dexamethasone 4 mg PO every morning for appetite stimulation (also may help fatigue). Limit to 2-4 week course given diabetes.
   - Nutritional counseling referral placed by oncology
   - High-calorie oral supplements between meals
4. Insomnia/sleep disruption:
   - Melatonin 3 mg at bedtime
   - If pain is primary cause of awakening, optimizing analgesia should help
5. Mood/anxiety:
   - Supportive counseling today. Patient coping reasonably well with strong family support.
   - Will reassess at next visit. Consider low-dose SSRI if symptoms worsen.
6. Advance care planning:
   - Advance directive on file naming wife as healthcare proxy. Reviewed and confirmed today.
   - Will revisit goals of care periodically as disease evolves.
7. Follow up in 2 weeks, sooner if symptoms escalate. Available by phone for urgent symptom management questions.
